confidence.disclosure: Almost everything we know about Rabia comes from devotional biographies written centuries after her death — most famously Farid al-Din Attar's Tadhkirat al-Awliya in the early 13th century. The historical kernel (a freed slave woman of Basra who became the most famous female mystic of early Islam, who taught the doctrine of pure disinterested love of God, and who died around 801) is reasonably secure. Specific dated incidents are mostly legendary. Treat all dates as devotional rather than historical.

sources: Margaret Smith, Rabi'a the Mystic and her Fellow-Saints in Islam (1928)
Annemarie Schimmel, Mystical Dimensions of Islam (1975)
Rkia E. Cornell, Rabi'a from Narrative to Myth (2019)

journey.event: Born in Basra into a poor family, the fourth daughter — her name Rabi'a means 'fourth'. Hagiographies report that her parents had nothing in the house the night of her birth, not even oil for the lamp.
Loses both parents to a famine in Basra. She is separated from her sisters in the chaos and falls into the hands of a slave-trader who sells her into bondage.
Lives as a household slave in Basra. Hagiographies report that her master, observing a column of light over her head as she prayed at night, freed her in fear and reverence.

Withdraws to the desert outside Basra to live as an ascetic. Later traditions place a brief period of work as a flute-player or singer before her final renunciation, but the desert seclusion becomes her settled life.

Returns to Basra and lives in a small hut on the outskirts of the city, devoting herself to prayer, fasting, and the worship of God for God's own sake — not from fear of hell or hope of paradise. Her famous prayer with a torch in one hand and a bucket of water in the other ('to burn paradise and quench hell') becomes a defining image of disinterested love.

Becomes the spiritual centre of a circle that includes the great Hasan al-Basri (in legend at least, though Hasan died when Rabia was a child) and other ascetics of Basra. She refuses repeated offers of marriage, including one from the governor of Basra, saying that her existence is wholly absorbed in God.

Composes — in the words preserved by later Sufis — some of the earliest love poetry to God in Arabic. The lines 'I love Thee with two loves' become a touchstone of the entire Sufi tradition of divine love (mahabba).

Lives into old age in extreme poverty, refusing all gifts from admirers. According to Attar, when a wealthy follower offered her gold she replied that she was ashamed to ask anything from God in this world for herself, let alone from people.

Dies in Basra and is buried there. Her tomb becomes a place of visitation; later tradition transfers a tomb-shrine to the Mount of Olives in Jerusalem, but the historical Rabia almost certainly never left Iraq.
